questions.prompt: You add `cache_control` to your system prompt but see no cost reduction. `cache_read_input_tokens` is always 0. What's likely wrong?

choices.text: You need to pay extra to enable caching.

Something is varying in the cached prefix — most often a timestamp, user ID, or request ID being templated into the system prompt. Cache hits require byte-identical prefixes; one character of variation invalidates everything after it. Strip dynamic content from the cached region.

questions.explanation: Cache miss almost always means non-deterministic prefix. Common culprits: timestamps, dates rendered to strings, user-specific data injected into 'stable' content. Make the cached prefix deterministic — push all variable data into the messages array after the cached blocks.

questions.prompt: Roughly how much does Claude charge for input tokens served from the prompt cache vs. fresh input?

choices.text: About 10% of fresh-input price. Cache writes cost slightly more than fresh input (one-time), but every subsequent hit pays ~10% — typically translating to 70–90% cost reductions on chat/agent/RAG workloads when applied to system prompts, tool definitions, examples, and stable documents.

questions.explanation: Cache reads on Claude are roughly 10% of normal input pricing. Writes cost slightly more than fresh input (one-time). On any workload with a stable prefix and decent volume, this is the highest-leverage cost optimization available — typically a one-line change for 70–90% bill reduction.
